title: Origami samurai hat
subtitle: Folding the traditional kabuto helmet from a single square sheet
excerpt: The origami kabuto (samurai helmet) is a traditional Japanese paper fold that can be made wearable when folded from a large sheet of newspaper or wrapping paper. Folded at standard origami scale (15 cm), it produces a decorative desk ornament. The fold uses a fish-base variant and is one of the classic introductory models in Japanese origami teaching.
sourceNotes: The origami kabuto is a traditional Japanese paper fold documented in multiple origami collections throughout the twentieth century. The model predates modern origami notation and appears in Akira Yoshizawa and subsequent works. No single pre-1928 English-language source exists. Synthesised from contemporary origami documentation.

glossaryTerms.definition: The Japanese word for a samurai battle helmet, and the name of the traditional origami helmet model folded from a single square sheet.
A fold that brings all four corners of a square sheet to the centre point, producing a smaller square. Named after the folded pastry of the same shape.

content.text: begins with a square sheet folded in half diagonally and proceeds through a series of valley folds that raise the ear guards and form the brow plate. The model opens at the base to form a hollow that sits on a flat surface or on a small head.
A square sheet: origami paper at 15 cm produces a small ornament; a 60 cm square of newspaper produces a helmet that fits a child. The fold scales with the sheet.
Traditional kabuto are folded from printed newspaper or large sheets of decorative paper. If using patterned paper, start with the patterned side down so the exterior of the helmet shows the pattern.
Start with the square oriented as a diamond (a point at the top). Fold the bottom point up to the top point. You now have a triangle with the open edge at the top and the fold at the bottom.
Fold the left point diagonally up and to the right so it meets the top point. Repeat on the right side. You now have a smaller diamond with two wing points at the top.
Fold each wing point outward and slightly downward to form the ear guards. The angle of this fold sets the width of the brim; fold at approximately 45° to the centre line.
Fold the front flap of the bottom point upward to meet the centre, forming the first brow plate layer. Then fold it up again to create the double brow plate.
on the back flap: fold the back bottom point down and tuck it under the brow plate to lock the form.
Open the base of the helmet by gently spreading the two layers apart from the bottom opening.

Press the folded edges sharply with the bone folder or a fingernail for a crisp finish. The ear guards can be angled further forward or backward after folding to adjust the silhouette. The completed kabuto sits flat on a surface with the brow plate facing forward.
